units.name: Anomaly overview
units.reasoning:
Step 0 - Purpose. This is the framing step, not a decision step. It runs on every case to build the candidate picture and choose what to fetch next. It never emits a final (component, reason); it hands off to a KPI-specific unit once a candidate's faulted signal is identified.

Procedure.
1. Bind <metric_anomaly_events> to every metric_anomaly line accumulated so far. Read each event's fields: service, kpi (cpu | mem | diskio | socket | latency.p50 | latency.p90), z (robust deviation = (value-median)/(1.4826*MAD)), dir (up|down), n_windows (length of the consecutive anomalous run), persistent (n_windows>=2), and the peak-vs-base gap.
2. Group the bound events by (service, kpi). One service can carry several kpis; keep them together so co-occurring signals on one service are seen as a single fault picture, not several candidates.
3. Restrict to candidates: bind <candidate_components> to the possible_root_cause_components list (the 5 Online Boutique services: checkoutservice, currencyservice, emailservice, productcatalogservice, recommendationservice). Keep ONLY these as root-cause candidates. Every other service that appears in events (frontend, redis, cartservice, loadgenerator, paymentservice, shippingservice, adservice) is a supporting or propagation node and can NEVER be the final component, however large its deviation.
4. For each candidate, note its deviating LOCAL signal(s) with (|z|, dir, n_windows, persistent). Local means measured on that service itself, never inferred from a trace edge. Mark a kpi as strong when it is upward (dir=up) AND persistent (n_windows>=2 across consecutive windows).
5. Rank candidates provisionally by (persistence x |z|) on their strongest local kpi, but only within comparable scales - see discrimination.
6. Decide the next fetch for the top candidate: metric_summary(service,kpi) for the trajectory (onset time, step vs monotonic shape, magnitude vs baseline), span_slowdown(service) to see if latency concentrates on edges INTO it, log_pattern(service) for timing/impact.

Discrimination rules.
- z is only comparable WITHIN the same kpi/service scale. A socket |z| of 30 and a cpu |z| of 300 are NOT on the same footing (socket counts are small integers; cpu is a utilization ratio). Never rank across services or kpis by raw |z| alone.
- diskio and socket baselines are near zero/constant, so a modest sustained deviation there is a decisive injected-resource fingerprint that can outrank a numerically larger cpu z.
- A near-flat baseline yields MAD~0, so a tiny bump produces a huge z (tight-MAD artifact). For mem-like kpis always sanity-check peak vs base; a huge z with peak ~ base (ratio near 1.0) is weak.
- Upward (dir=up) resource/latency deviations are candidate faults; downward moves are throughput/GC/load-drop effects, not causes.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT promote a non-candidate just because its |z| is the largest in the case. If redis shows mem z=+900 (a classic drift offender) while productcatalogservice shows a persistent local cpu z=+41 across 6 windows, the candidate cpu wins: redis cannot be the component. Likewise, do NOT stop here and declare a winner from the overview alone - a single-window spike (persistent=false) must be confirmed with a trajectory fetch first.

Worked example. Accumulated metric_anomaly events: M0012 redis mem z=+880 dir=up (NON-candidate, drop for ranking); M0031 checkoutservice cpu z=+38 dir=up persistent n_windows=6; M0044 checkoutservice latency.p90 z=+12 dir=up persistent; M0058 currencyservice latency.p50 z=+5 dir=up n_windows=2. Grouping: checkoutservice has {cpu z=+38 (6w), latency.p90 z=+12} - one candidate with a strong resource kpi plus a co-moving latency (likely its effect); currencyservice has {latency.p50 z=+5} - a candidate with only local latency and no resource kpi. Provisional rank1 = checkoutservice (strong persistent cpu); rank2 = currencyservice (latency-only). Next fetch: metric_summary(checkoutservice,cpu) to confirm the run is sustained, and span_slowdown(currencyservice) to see if latency flows into it.

Cross-modal validation. Nothing is concluded here; the overview only tells you which candidate to probe and with what. Confirm a resource candidate with its own metric_summary trajectory and with inbound span_slowdown/log timing before you let a specific unit set the reason.

units.name: CPU anomaly
units.reasoning:
Step - CPU saturation localization. Fires when a candidate service carries an upward cpu metric_anomaly with severity |z|>=3. Goal: decide whether service_cpu is the injected fault, or whether cpu is a side effect of another injected resource on the same service.

Procedure.
1. Bind <cpu_metric_events> to the cpu metric_anomaly lines and <other_service_metric_events> to the metric_anomaly lines on all OTHER services. Read for the candidate: z, dir, n_windows, persistent, peak vs base.
2. Confirm the run is sustained (C1). A cpu fault in this benchmark spans the whole post-injection region - many consecutive 30s windows. If persistent=false or n_windows is small, treat it as a transient and request metric_summary(service,cpu) before trusting it; a lone anomalous window is not a fault.
3. Read magnitude in context (C2). cpu is a utilization ratio; injected cpu stress commonly drives z into the hundreds. Compare against the SAME service's other kpis to judge which is primary - do not compare its cpu z against another service's cpu z to rank across services.
4. Check for a masking injected resource on the SAME service (C3). If that candidate ALSO shows an upward diskio or socket anomaly, cpu is very likely a side effect (IO-wait for diskio; spin/retry for socket) and the injected resource is diskio/socket. Switch the reason to that resource and rank cpu below it.
5. Verify downstream effects (C4). A real cpu saturation co-raises the service's own latency.p90, produces inbound span_slowdown edges (callers waiting on it), and may drop its log throughput. Presence strengthens service_cpu; total absence over many windows hints the cpu anomaly is measurement noise (often on a non-candidate).
6. Rank (C5). If cpu is the strongest sustained LOCAL signal on a candidate with no injected-IO/socket competitor, set rank1 = service_cpu and place its mem/latency below as effects.

Discrimination and the resource-over-symptom rule. A co-located cpu anomaly OUTRANKS the latency it causes: when the same candidate shows both cpu and latency rising, the reason is cpu, and latency is the downstream symptom. But cpu itself is OUTRANKED by a co-located diskio or socket signal, because those are near-zero in baseline and are the distinctive injected fingerprints; cpu is then their IO-wait/spin effect.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT switch the reason to latency just because the service shows a large trace p99. Under cpu saturation, latency (local and edge) is the symptom - keep the reason cpu. Conversely, do NOT keep cpu as rank1 when the same service has a persistent socket rise: the classic mistake is 'cpu z is 10x socket z so it must be cpu' - the injected resource is socket, and the big cpu is its effect.

Worked example. M0031 checkoutservice cpu z=+38 dir=up persistent n_windows=6; M0044 checkoutservice latency.p90 z=+12 dir=up persistent; <other_service_metric_events> near baseline (currencyservice cpu z=+1.2, emailservice cpu z=-0.4). No diskio/socket anomaly on checkoutservice. Because cpu is the strongest sustained LOCAL signal and is co-located with the latency it causes, reason = cpu, NOT latency; rank1 = checkoutservice_cpu, with latency.p90 noted as its downstream symptom. Contrast: if the same case also had M0090 checkoutservice socket z=+9 persistent n_windows=6, the reason would flip to socket (injected resource), demoting cpu to an effect.

Cross-modal validation. Confirm with metric_summary(checkoutservice,cpu) showing a sustained elevation over the injection region; with span_slowdown edges INTO checkoutservice (e.g. frontend>checkoutservice PlaceOrder p99 up) which is expected propagation of the local slowdown; and with a log_pattern throughput drop on checkoutservice during the same window. These validate impact and timing - they do not by themselves change the reason from cpu.

units.name: Memory anomaly
units.reasoning:
Step - Memory growth localization. Fires when a candidate carries an UPWARD mem metric_anomaly (dir=up) with severity |z|>=3. Goal: confirm a genuine sustained memory increase (step allocation or monotonic leak) and set service_mem, while rejecting tight-MAD artifacts and downward moves.

Procedure.
1. Bind <mem_metric_events> to the upward mem metric_anomaly lines on candidates. Read z, dir, n_windows, persistent, peak, base.
2. Discard downward mem (M1). A dir=down mem move is GC reclaiming or load falling off - never a memory fault. Exclude it from the candidate reason even if |z| is large.
3. Reality-check the magnitude (M2). mem is measured in bytes. Compute the ratio peak/base. A real fault shows peak clearly above base - often >1.3x for a plateau, many-fold for a leak. A large z with peak ~ base (ratio near 1.0) is a tight-MAD artifact from an almost-flat baseline (MAD~0 inflates z); downgrade it and prefer a rival candidate that has a genuine relative change.
4. Classify the trajectory (M3) via metric_summary(service,mem): a STEP change (jump to a plateau) indicates a sudden allocation; a MONOTONIC climb indicates a leak. Either shape supports service_mem; a spike that returns to baseline does not.
5. Corroborate cross-modally (M4): allocation/OOM/out-of-memory warning logs, a new_template appearing after onset, or restart-like gaps in metrics all reinforce a mem fault.
6. Rank (M5). A genuine sustained upward mem with a real peak-vs-base gap plus one corroboration -> rank1 = service_mem.

Discrimination rules.
- Persistence AND a real gap are both required. Persistence alone with peak ~ base is the tight-MAD trap.
- mem z is not comparable to cpu z across kpis; judge mem on its own peak/base ratio and trajectory, not by comparing its z to a cpu z on another service.
- If the same candidate also shows a persistent diskio or socket anomaly, reconsider: those near-zero-baseline injected signals normally take precedence, and mem may be incidental. But a clear monotonic mem climb with a large real gap is itself a strong injected signature and can be rank1.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT rank a non-candidate's mem drift - redis is the frequent offender, often showing the largest mem z in the whole case; it can never be the component. And do NOT trust a huge mem z when peak/base ~ 1.0: that is a flat-baseline artifact, not growth. Example of the trap: M0007 emailservice mem z=+210 but peak=512.3MB base=511.9MB (ratio 1.0008) - reject as tight-MAD, do not make it rank1.

Worked example. M0025 productcatalogservice mem z=+46 dir=up persistent n_windows=8, peak=1.42GB base=380MB (ratio 3.7x). metric_summary(productcatalogservice,mem) shows a monotonic climb from onset (leak shape). L0071 productcatalogservice new_template=true level=warn 'allocation' after onset. Genuine sustained growth + real gap + log corroboration -> rank1 = productcatalogservice_mem. If a rival M0031 checkoutservice cpu z=+38 also exists, mem still stands as its own candidate's reason on productcatalogservice; the two are different candidates and are compared in the final rerank by strength of local evidence.

Cross-modal validation. The confirming signals are: metric_summary showing step/monotonic shape with peak >> base; log_pattern with allocation/OOM warnings or a new_template at onset; possibly a restart gap. Traces (span_slowdown/error_code) mostly show downstream impact of GC pauses and validate timing, not the reason. Logs and traces confirm - they never override a genuine local mem metric, and never manufacture a mem fault from a tight-MAD z.

units.name: Disk IO anomaly
units.reasoning:
Step - Disk IO localization. Fires when a candidate carries an UPWARD diskio metric_anomaly (dir=up) with severity |z|>=3. Goal: recognize the near-zero-baseline diskio jump as a decisive injected-resource fingerprint and set service_diskio, even when cpu and latency also move on that service.

Procedure.
1. Bind <diskio_metric_events> to the upward diskio metric_anomaly lines on candidates. Read z, dir, n_windows, persistent, peak, base.
2. Trust the magnitude here (D1). diskio baseline is near zero for most services, so a sustained jump to large bytes/s is a genuine injected-resource fingerprint. Unlike mem, the large z is REAL because the baseline truly was near zero - there is no tight-MAD ambiguity to worry about in the same way; the discriminator is that base ~ 0 and peak is large and sustained.
3. Confirm persistence (D2) via metric_summary(service,diskio). A disk fault runs for many consecutive windows; a single-window blip is not enough.
4. Apply injected-resource precedence (D3). Co-moving cpu (from IO-wait) and latency (from blocked IO) are EFFECTS. Do NOT let a co-located cpu with a numerically larger z override diskio as the reason. The near-zero-baseline diskio is the injected signal; cpu/latency sit below as consequences.
5. Rank (D4). Once persistence is confirmed, rank1 = service_diskio, with cpu and latency listed as effects.

Discrimination rules.
- Cross-scale z is meaningless here in the usual way: cpu z of +300 does not beat diskio z of +40 when diskio's baseline was ~0. The distinctive baseline, not the raw z, decides.
- Persistence is the confirmation: prefer a sustained run over a one-window spike; fetch the trajectory if in doubt.
- diskio, like socket, is a resource that beats cpu/latency in the resource-over-symptom ordering. Among diskio/socket vs cpu/mem, the near-zero-baseline resources (diskio, socket) take precedence when sustained.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT demote diskio to secondary just because cpu z is larger - this is the exact common mistake for disk faults. If checkoutservice shows cpu z=+120 and diskio z=+35, the injected fault is diskio (base ~ 0, sustained); cpu is IO-wait. Also do NOT read a downward diskio move as a fault, and do NOT elevate a non-candidate's disk activity (loadgenerator, redis) to the component.

Worked example. M0052 currencyservice diskio z=+34 dir=up persistent n_windows=7, peak=48MB/s base=0.05MB/s (base essentially zero, large sustained peak); M0053 currencyservice cpu z=+96 dir=up persistent; M0060 currencyservice latency.p90 z=+15 dir=up. Despite cpu z (+96) being far larger than diskio z (+34), the near-zero-baseline diskio is the injected resource: reason = diskio, rank1 = currencyservice_diskio; cpu (+96) is IO-wait and latency (+15) is the blocked-IO symptom, both ranked below as effects.

Cross-modal validation. Confirm with metric_summary(currencyservice,diskio) showing a sustained plateau far above a ~0 baseline; with inbound span_slowdown (e.g. frontend>currencyservice or checkoutservice>currencyservice edges slow) validating that the slowdown originates at currencyservice; and with log_pattern timing aligning to the window. These validate impact/timing; the reason stays diskio, fixed by the local near-zero-baseline metric.

units.name: Socket anomaly
units.reasoning:
Step - Socket exhaustion localization. Fires when a candidate carries an UPWARD socket metric_anomaly (dir=up) with severity |z|>=3. Goal: recognize a persistent socket rise as service_socket, and correctly treat co-located cpu on the SAME service as supporting evidence rather than a competing reason.

Procedure.
1. Bind <socket_metric_events> to the upward socket metric_anomaly lines on candidates and <same_service_cpu_events> to cpu anomalies on those SAME candidates. Read z, dir, n_windows, persistent.
2. Judge by PERSISTENCE, not magnitude (S1). socket counts are small integers with a nearly constant baseline, so even a real fault yields a modest z. A persistent socket rise across many consecutive windows is the injected signal; do not dismiss it because its z is small.
3. Read cpu as SUPPORT, not competition (S2). Socket exhaustion makes the service spin/retry, which raises cpu. A large co-located cpu therefore REINFORCES socket rather than outranking it. Bind <same_service_cpu_events> and note them as the effect of socket pressure.
4. Corroborate cross-modally (S3): connection-refused / retry / timeout logs, or error_code bursts on inbound edges, confirm socket exhaustion.
5. Rank (S4). rank1 = service_socket for a persistent socket rise even when the co-located cpu |z| is far larger; cpu goes below as an effect.

Discrimination rules.
- socket and diskio are the two near-zero/near-constant-baseline resources; a sustained deviation is a decisive injected fingerprint that outranks cpu and latency in the resource-over-symptom ordering.
- Because socket z is inherently modest, NEVER compare it to a cpu z to decide which is primary. The comparison is scale-invalid; persistence decides.
- Downward socket moves are not faults (connections closing). Only sustained upward rises count.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT choose cpu because 'cpu z is 10x socket z' - this is the single most common error on socket faults. If a candidate shows socket z=+8 persistent (n_windows=6) and cpu z=+140 persistent, the injected resource is socket; the cpu is spin/retry. Also do NOT attribute the socket fault to a non-candidate that merely shows connection churn (loadgenerator); the component must be one of the 5 candidates.

Worked example. M0088 recommendationservice socket z=+9 dir=up persistent n_windows=6, peak=range from a small-integer baseline; M0089 recommendationservice cpu z=+130 dir=up persistent n_windows=6 (bound into <same_service_cpu_events>); L0102 recommendationservice level=error 'connection refused'/'retry' new_template=true. Even though cpu z (+130) dwarfs socket z (+9), the persistent socket rise on a near-constant baseline is the injected resource: reason = socket, rank1 = recommendationservice_socket; the large cpu is the spin/retry effect that CONFIRMS socket, and the connection-refused logs corroborate.

Cross-modal validation. The confirming signals for socket are: log_pattern with connection-refused/retry/timeout templates or a new_template at onset on the same service; error_code bursts on inbound edges (callers seeing failures as sockets exhaust); and metric_summary(service,socket) showing the sustained small-integer rise. The co-located cpu in <same_service_cpu_events> is itself corroboration, not a rival reason. Logs and traces confirm timing/impact; they never flip the reason away from socket to cpu.

units.name: Latency propagation vs locality
units.reasoning:
Step - Latency: root cause vs propagation. Fires when latency signals appear on a candidate: local latency.p50/p90 metric_anomaly and/or edge span_slowdown (latency.p99). Goal: localize a genuine latency fault (delay/loss) by the candidate's OWN latency metric, and NOT be misled by large caller-edge p99 that is only propagation.

Procedure.
1. Separate the two signal types. Bind <latency_metric_events> to the LOCAL latency metric_anomaly lines (kpi latency.p50 / latency.p90 - measured on the service itself). Bind <span_slowdown_events> to the EDGE slowdowns (kpi latency.p99 = RAW span duration on a caller>callee/operation). Bind <call_edge_events> to the call graph structure.
2. Understand p99 semantics (L3). span_slowdown p99 is RAW span duration, which INCLUDES downstream wait. So a slow CALLER edge is EXPECTED propagation, not proof the caller is at fault. In Online Boutique, PlaceOrder (frontend>checkoutservice) and its children always have the largest raw p99 whenever ANY downstream dependency is slow - that magnitude is propagation.
3. Localize with the LOCAL metric (L1). Find the candidate whose OWN latency.p50/p90 metric z is highest. The per-service latency metric is the clean localizer; the edge p99 is not.
4. Rule out a resource cause (L2). If that candidate has ANY co-located cpu/mem/diskio/socket anomaly, its latency is a SYMPTOM of that resource - hand off to the resource/injected unit; the reason becomes the resource, not latency. Only when there is NO local resource anomaly is service_latency (a delay/loss fault) the reason.
5. Confirm direction with traces (L3). Real latency faults concentrate span_slowdown on edges INTO the candidate (it is the slow callee). Verify the slowdown flows toward, not away from, your candidate.
6. Handle loss faults (L4). For loss, follow error_code down the graph to the deepest failing callee; the reason stays latency, located by the cascade.
7. Rank (L5). rank1 = service_latency when the LOCAL latency metric leads, no resource anomaly exists on that candidate, and slowdown/errors flow into it.

Discrimination rules.
- Never rank a service on edge p99 alone. Localize by local latency.p50/p90. Do not move rank1 to a caller because its edge p99 is large.
- Resource-over-symptom: a co-located cpu/mem/diskio/socket on the candidate demotes latency to a symptom.
- A candidate with local latency and NO resource anomaly is the clean latency signature.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT rank checkoutservice (or frontend, a non-candidate) as the root cause just because frontend>checkoutservice PlaceOrder shows the biggest p99 in the trace. That p99 is the sum of downstream waits. If the actual delay is in currencyservice, its LOCAL latency.p90 metric is what points there. Conversely, do NOT call something service_latency when the same service also has a persistent cpu/socket/diskio anomaly - then latency is the effect and the resource is the reason.

Worked example. S0021 frontend>checkoutservice PlaceOrder p99=880ms baseline=120ms z=+30 (huge, but a CALLER edge = propagation); S0022 checkoutservice>currencyservice Convert p99=610ms z=+22; M0067 currencyservice latency.p90 z=+18 dir=up persistent n_windows=5 (LOCAL); no cpu/mem/diskio/socket anomaly on currencyservice. Localizing by the local metric, the delay lives in currencyservice: reason = latency, rank1 = currencyservice_latency. The frontend>checkoutservice p99 (+30) is NOT evidence against currencyservice - it is the propagated wait; checkoutservice is not moved to rank1 for it. Had currencyservice also shown diskio z=+34 persistent, the reason would flip to diskio (resource-over-symptom).

Cross-modal validation. Confirm with span_slowdown concentrating on edges INTO currencyservice (checkoutservice>currencyservice, frontend>currencyservice) rather than on its outbound edges; with error_code on inbound edges if it is a loss fault; and with log_pattern timing on currencyservice matching the window. The local latency.p50/p90 metric remains the localizer; traces confirm the direction of propagation but never make a caller the root cause.

units.name: Error code cascade
units.reasoning:
Step - Error-code cascade to the deepest callee. Fires when error_code events appear involving a candidate. Goal: attribute non-OK statuses to the deepest/earliest FAILING callee, not to the callers that merely relay the failure, and remember that errors validate impact/timing but do NOT by themselves set the reason.

Procedure.
1. Bind <error_code_events> to the error_code lines (fields: caller, callee, operation, error_count, call_count, status_codes) and <call_edge_events> to the static call graph (caller>callee structure).
2. Walk the errors DOWN the graph. Errors cascade upward from a failing dependency: the callee fails, and every caller above it reports non-OK because its downstream call failed. Follow the edges from the top-level failing caller toward the leaves.
3. Identify the deepest/earliest callee that emits non-OK on its OWN outbound behavior (or is the terminal failing node). Attribute the failure there. The relaying callers above it are propagation nodes.
4. Keep the component within the 5 candidates. If the deepest failing node is a non-candidate (e.g. paymentservice, cartservice), map to the nearest candidate implicated by local metric evidence; a non-candidate can never be the final component.
5. Use errors for impact/timing only. The reason is still set from the candidate's component-LOCAL metric evidence (cpu/mem/diskio/socket/latency). Errors confirm that the fault caused failures in a time window; they do not name the fault type.

Discrimination rules.
- Direction: an error on edge A>B where A is a caller means B (or something below B) is failing; do not blame A.
- A high error_count on a top-of-graph edge (frontend>checkoutservice) is the aggregate of downstream failures - it is propagation, mirroring how caller-edge p99 is propagation for latency.
- status_codes hint at the fault family (e.g. resource-exhausted/unavailable for socket exhaustion; deadline-exceeded for latency/loss), but they corroborate, they do not override the local metric.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT attribute the fault to checkoutservice just because frontend>checkoutservice PlaceOrder shows the largest error_count. That is the cascade sum. If the real failure is emailservice returning non-OK on checkoutservice>emailservice SendOrderConfirmation, the attribution belongs to emailservice (a candidate), located by its own metric. Also do NOT let errors, on their own, set reason=latency or reason=socket - always confirm the reason with local metric evidence.

Worked example. E0011 frontend>checkoutservice PlaceOrder error_count=210 status=UNAVAILABLE (top of cascade); E0012 checkoutservice>emailservice SendOrderConfirmation error_count=205 status=RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED (deeper); no failing edge below emailservice. Following the cascade to the deepest failing callee -> emailservice is the failing node. It is a candidate, so component = emailservice. The reason is NOT set by the errors: check emailservice's local metrics - if M0044 emailservice socket z=+9 persistent, reason = socket (RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED corroborates); if instead only emailservice latency.p90 is up with no resource anomaly, reason = latency (loss). The frontend>checkoutservice errors are propagation and do not make checkoutservice the component.

Cross-modal validation. Confirm the failing node with: its own metric_anomaly (the reason source); span_slowdown on inbound edges to it aligning with the error window; and log_pattern on that service showing error-level templates or a new_template at onset. The error cascade fixes WHERE (deepest failing callee, mapped to a candidate) and WHEN (impact window); the local metric fixes WHY (the reason).

units.name: Log confirmation
units.reasoning:
Step - Log confirmation of the leading candidate. Fires when log_pattern events are available. Goal: use logs to CONFIRM timing and impact for the leading candidate and its neighbors, using compact fields only, without ever overriding strong component-local metric evidence and without copying raw template text.

Procedure.
1. Bind <same_service_log_events> to log_pattern lines on the leading candidate and <neighbor_log_events> to log_pattern lines on its call-graph neighbors. Use ONLY compact fields: id, template_id, level, z (per-template count deviation), new_template. Never copy raw template text into findings.
2. Read the candidate's logs for confirming signatures: level=error or level=warn templates in the anomaly window; new_template=true appearing after onset (a fault often triggers a never-before-seen line); or a throughput drop shown as a negative count z on normal templates (the service is doing less work because it is saturated/blocked).
3. Match the log timing to the metric anomaly window. A confirming log must fall in the same window as the metric evidence; a warning from before injection or unrelated to the window is not confirmation.
4. Map log signatures to the suspected reason as CORROBORATION: allocation/OOM -> mem; connection-refused/retry/timeout -> socket; deadline-exceeded/slow -> latency/loss; IO/disk errors -> diskio; high CPU/throttle or a throughput drop -> cpu. This raises confidence in a reason already set by metrics; it does not set the reason alone.
5. Read neighbor logs for propagation context only: a downstream neighbor's error logs help confirm the cascade direction, but the component stays the candidate localized by metrics.

Discrimination rules.
- Logs CONFIRM or ADD SUPPORT; they NEVER override strong local metric evidence. If metrics say diskio and logs are quiet, the reason stays diskio.
- A new_template or error log on a NON-candidate is context, not a component decision.
- A negative count z (throughput drop) is an impact signal consistent with saturation - it corroborates, it is not itself a resource reason.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT flip the reason to match a log when metrics disagree: if productcatalogservice has a genuine mem climb (peak >> base) but its logs only show a generic timeout template, do NOT change reason to latency because of the log - the local mem metric wins. And do NOT paste raw template strings; reference template_id and level only.

Worked example. Leading candidate recommendationservice (metrics point to socket). <same_service_log_events>: L0102 template_id=T44 level=error new_template=true (onset-aligned), L0103 template_id=T09 level=info z=-6 (throughput drop). <neighbor_log_events>: L0110 productcatalogservice template_id=T12 level=warn z=+4. The onset-aligned error new_template T44 plus the throughput drop on recommendationservice confirm timing and impact for the socket hypothesis; if T44 corresponds to connection-refused/retry, it corroborates socket specifically. This RAISES confidence in reason=socket but does not change it; the neighbor warn on productcatalogservice is propagation context, not a component switch.

Cross-modal validation. Logs are themselves the confirmation layer here; they should agree with the metric-derived reason and with the error/trace timing. When logs, metrics, and traces all point to the same window and same service, confidence is high. When logs are silent but a near-zero-baseline diskio/socket metric is persistent, keep the metric-derived reason - absence of a log does not weaken a strong local metric. Never let a log override metrics, and never surface raw log text.

units.name: Final rerank
units.reasoning:
Step - Final reason reranker. Fires in the final phase. Goal: consolidate all accumulated evidence into exactly three ranked (component, reason) pairs, keeping the candidate with the strongest, most persistent component-LOCAL metric evidence and setting each reason from the strongest LOCAL signal, honoring the resource-over-symptom and near-zero-baseline doctrines.

Procedure.
1. Bind <accumulated_candidate_rankings> to the provisional candidate list gathered across the investigation and <selected_component_metric_evidence> to the per-kpi local evidence (cpu, mem, diskio, socket, latency) for the leading candidate.
2. Keep the component with the strongest, MOST PERSISTENT component-local metric evidence, UNLESS a rival candidate has strictly stronger local evidence. Persistence (n_windows, persistent=true, sustained trajectory) counts more than a single large spike.
3. Set the reason for the selected component from its strongest LOCAL signal, applying the arbitration order when several kpis moved on it:
   (a) a persistent diskio or socket signal (near-zero/near-constant baseline) OUTRANKS a co-moving cpu or latency - these are the distinctive injected fingerprints;
   (b) else a persistent cpu or mem with a genuine relative change (peak >> base, not tight-MAD) is the reason;
   (c) else, if only latency is local and NO resource anomaly exists on that candidate, the reason is latency.
   Everything else on that service is a downstream effect, ranked below, never as its reason.
4. Downgrade a latency reason that is supported MAINLY by trace impact, downstream latency, or neighbor symptoms rather than by the candidate's own latency.p50/p90 metric. Edge p99 (RAW duration) is propagation and is not local evidence.
5. Reject non-candidates entirely: a non-candidate service (frontend, redis, cartservice, loadgenerator, paymentservice, shippingservice, adservice) can never be a component, however large its z or error_count.
6. Output the top-3 as (component, reason) pairs drawn only from the candidate list and the {cpu, mem, diskio, socket, latency} reason set, each with a short justification citing event ids.

Discrimination rules.
- Cross-scale z is not a ranking key: a socket z of +9 with a near-constant baseline beats a co-moving cpu z of +140 on the same service.
- A tight-MAD mem z (peak ~ base) is weak and must not be rank1 over a genuine sustained rival.
- Persistence and a real peak-vs-base gap distinguish a fault from a baseline artifact or a transient spike.

Counter-example / failure mode. Do NOT rank1 a candidate on cpu when the same candidate has a persistent diskio/socket signal - the near-zero-baseline resource is the injected one and cpu is its effect. Do NOT rank1 a caller (or a non-candidate) for a huge edge p99 - that is propagation. Do NOT let logs or errors, which only confirm timing/impact, set a reason that the local metrics do not support.

Worked example. <accumulated_candidate_rankings>: [currencyservice (diskio z=+34 persistent n_windows=7, base~0; cpu z=+96; latency.p90 z=+15), checkoutservice (cpu z=+38 persistent; latency.p90 z=+12), emailservice (mem z=+210 but peak~base tight-MAD)]. Rerank: currencyservice has the strongest, most persistent, near-zero-baseline diskio signal -> rank1 = (currencyservice, diskio); its cpu/latency are effects. checkoutservice has a genuine persistent cpu with co-located latency as symptom -> rank2 = (checkoutservice, cpu). emailservice's mem is a tight-MAD artifact (peak~base) -> demote; rank3 = (checkoutservice, latency) as a lower-confidence alternative OR (emailservice, mem) only if peak/base proves genuine on re-check. Each pair cites its event ids (M0052/M0053/M0060 for currencyservice; M0031/M0044 for checkoutservice).

Cross-modal validation. The final ranking should be internally consistent: the rank1 reason is a persistent LOCAL metric; span_slowdown flows INTO the rank1 service; error_code cascades terminate at (or map to) it; and log_pattern timing aligns. If traces/logs contradict a purely metric-driven pick, re-examine the localization - but a strong, persistent, near-zero-baseline local metric is authoritative and is never overturned by propagation-side trace/log symptoms.
